ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt; mapped to closest parent class 'cooked vegetable food product' (FOODON_03315091). The caramelized dipping sauce component containing pork belly and dried shrimp is not captured by this class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific entry for Vietnamese boiled vegetables with caramelized pork-shrimp dipping sauce. Code A00LC (cooked vegetables) used as the closest defensible anchor; the sauce component is not fully represented.

main_ingredients: assorted fresh vegetables (e.g. water spinach, long beans, okra, broccoli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g (1 plate), comprising a generous portion of boiled mixed vegetables accompanied by a small bowl of caramelized pork-and-shrimp dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Vegetables, mixed, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (SR Legacy) and Pork, fresh, belly, raw (SR Legacy); composite estimation for Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for boiled mixed vegetables (FDC ID 170070), pork belly (FDC ID 168309), dried shrimp (FDC ID 175180), and fish sauce (FDC ID 173268), weighted to reflect a typical dish ratio of approximately 70% vegetables and 30% sauce (pork belly, dried shrimp, fish sauce, sugar). Input record values (energy 95 kcal, protein 4.5 g, fat 6.25 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g, sodium 450 mg) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained with medium confidence. Sodium of 450 mg/100g is plausible given fish sauce and dried shrimp content.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed boiled vegetables (GI ~38) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt. GI of 38 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed boiled vegetables (low-GI, approximately 35–40). The caramelized dipping sauce contributes added sugar (3 g/100g) which may marginally elevate the effective GI; however, the dominant vegetable component and low total carbohydrate (5 g/100g) keep the overall GI in the low range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (38 × 20.0) / 100 = 7.6, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 5.0 × 4 = 20.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg per 100 g, driven by fish sauce and dried shrimp in the caramelized dipping sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request the dipping sauce on the side and use it sparingly, as the sauce is the primary sodium source; the boiled vegetables themselves are low in sodium and beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (5 g/100g), low GI (~38), and high vegetable fibre base
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load and low glycaemic index; however, the added sugar in the caramelized dipping sauce should be used sparingly to avoid unnecessary glycaemic spikes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly (2.5 g/100g) and high sodium (450 mg/100g) in the dipping sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should limit the quantity of dipping sauce consumed to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake, while the boiled vegetable base provides beneficial dietary fibre and vitamin C that support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid from pork belly and high sodium from fish sauce and dried shrimp
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit the dipping sauce to reduce arachidonic acid and sodium intake, while the cruciferous vegetables and vitamin C in the dish provide anti-inflammatory antioxidant support.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt (Boiled Vegetables with Caramelized Dipping Sauce) is a traditional Vietnamese dish consisting of an assortment of fresh vegetables — commonly water spinach (rau muống), long beans, okra, and broccoli — that are briefly boiled until just tender and served alongside a rich, deeply savoury caramelized dipping sauce known as Kho Quẹt. The sauce is prepared by slowly rendering pork belly to release its fat, then sautéing shallots and garlic in the rendered fat, adding dried shrimp, fish sauce, and palm or white sugar, and simmering until the mixture thickens and caramelizes to a glossy, sticky consistency. The result is a contrast of clean, mild, slightly sweet boiled vegetables against an intensely umami, salty-sweet, and slightly smoky sauce. The dish is a staple of Southern Vietnamese home cooking and is commonly served as part of a shared family meal with steamed rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 95 kcal per 100 g, with 4.5 g protein, 6.25 g fat (2.5 g saturated), and only 5 g carbohydrate, making it relatively low in carbohydrates. Sodium is elevated at 450 mg/100 g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, which is a key clinical concern for hypertension. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 38 (low), and the glycaemic load per standard 400 g serving is approximately 7.6. The vegetable base contributes dietary fibre (1.5 g/100g), vitamin C (10 mg/100g), calcium (25 mg/100g), and iron (0.5 mg/100g). The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal diets without modification.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Rau Luộc Kho Quẹt exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values were estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients weighted by typical dish ratios. The input record values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed boiled vegetables. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile data for this composite Vietnamese dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not available for this composite dish in any approved source consulted. 3. Nutrient values are estimated composites; a laboratory analysis of a standardized recipe would be required for high-confidence values. 4. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' which appears to reflect the serving context (GrabFood Singapore) rather than the dish's true culinary origin; region_of_origin has been corrected to Vietnam. 5. The input ethnic_tags included 'Pho', 'Banh Mi', and 'Noodle' which are not relevant to this dish and have been removed. 6. The input source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' was listed under HPB Singapore but does not directly support the nutrient values for this dish; the nutrient source has been updated to reflect USDA FoodData Central composite estimation. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.03 g/100g is retained from the input record as a trace value consistent with pork belly content; this is a plausible trace amount. 8. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible approximation; no exact FoodEx2 code for this Vietnamese composite dish was identified. 9.
